intro:
Compound interest is the most powerful force in personal finance — it is the interest earned on both your original principal and the interest already accumulated. Unlike simple interest which only earns on the principal, compound interest grows exponentially over time, turning modest savings into substantial wealth with patience and consistency.

Albert Einstein famously called compound interest the eighth wonder of the world. A $10,000 investment at 7% annual interest compounded monthly grows to over $20,000 in 10 years and nearly $55,000 in 25 years — without adding a single extra dollar. This exponential growth is why starting to invest early is the single most impactful financial decision most people can make.

Our compound interest calculator shows exactly how your money grows with the formula A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt), where P is principal, r is annual rate, n is compounding frequency, and t is time in years. Use it to project investment growth, compare savings accounts, or understand the true cost of debt.
howItWorks: Enter your principal amount (initial investment), annual interest rate, number of years, and compounding frequency (daily, monthly, quarterly, or annually). The calculator applies the compound interest formula to show your final amount and total interest earned. Experiment with different rates and time periods to see how dramatically small changes affect the outcome.
interpretationGuide: The final amount shown is your total balance after the specified period. The total interest earned is the growth above your original principal. Notice how increasing the time period dramatically increases the final amount — this is the exponential nature of compounding at work.

faqs.question: What is compound interest?
faqs.answer: Compound interest is interest calculated on both the initial principal and the accumulated interest from previous periods. This causes the balance to grow exponentially rather than linearly, making it far more powerful than simple interest over long periods.

faqs.question: How often does interest compound?
faqs.answer: Common compounding frequencies include daily (365x/year), monthly (12x/year), quarterly (4x/year), and annually (1x/year). More frequent compounding results in slightly higher returns. Most savings accounts compound daily or monthly.

faqs.question: What is the Rule of 72?
faqs.answer: The Rule of 72 estimates how long it takes to double your money. Divide 72 by the annual interest rate. At 6%, your money doubles in 72/6 = 12 years. At 9%, it doubles in 8 years. It is a quick mental math shortcut for compound growth.

faqs.question: What interest rate should I use?
faqs.answer: High-yield savings accounts currently offer 4-5% APY. The US stock market has historically averaged 7-10% annually. Bonds typically return 3-5%. Use realistic rates for your specific investment type rather than optimistic projections.

faqs.question: What is the difference between APR and APY?
faqs.answer: APR (Annual Percentage Rate) is the stated interest rate without compounding. APY (Annual Percentage Yield) reflects compounding and is always equal to or higher than APR. When comparing accounts, compare APY for accurate comparison.

faqs.question: How does compounding help with long-term investing?
faqs.answer: Long time horizons magnify compounding dramatically. $5,000 at 8% for 10 years grows to $10,795. The same amount for 30 years grows to $50,313. Starting early — even with small amounts — has far more impact than investing larger amounts later.

faqs.question: Can compound interest work against me?
faqs.answer: Yes. Credit card debt compounds against you, often at 20-30% rates. A $5,000 credit card balance at 24% interest compounds to over $45,000 if left unpaid for 10 years. This is why paying off high-interest debt is the best guaranteed return.

faqs.question: What is continuous compounding?
faqs.answer: Continuous compounding is the theoretical limit where interest compounds infinitely frequently, calculated as A = Pe^(rt). In practice, daily compounding is nearly equivalent and is the most common real-world approximation of continuous compounding.

faqs.question: How does inflation affect compound returns?
faqs.answer: Inflation erodes purchasing power over time. If your investment earns 7% but inflation runs at 3%, your real return is approximately 4%. Always consider real (inflation-adjusted) returns when planning long-term goals.

faqs.question: Should I prioritize saving or investing for compound growth?
faqs.answer: High-yield savings accounts are best for emergency funds and short-term goals (1-5 years) at lower risk. Stock market investments are better for long-term goals (5+ years) where higher returns offset volatility through compounding.
